title: NVIDIA gates every agent action behind a pre-action policy check
summary: NVIDIA's Secure Agent Workspace reference design treats agent governance as a runtime control — default-deny networking, a credential proxy, signed policy bundles, and a policy check before every single action — a pattern AWS and Microsoft ship independently.
takeaways: Gate every state-changing agent action behind an inline policy check and a human approval.
Agents ship faster than the controls around them, so an audit log written after the fact can't stop a bad action — only a check before it can.
Default-deny the network, proxy credentials so the agent never sees raw secrets, deliver signed policy bundles, and verify them before each step.

sources.title: How to Govern Autonomous Agents in Enterprise AI Factories (NVIDIA, 2026-06-29)
Four Security Principles for Agentic AI Systems (AWS, 2026-04-02)
Governing the Agentic Enterprise (California Management Review, 2026-03-20)

body:
## What happened
On [2026-06-29](https://developer.nvidia.com/blog/how-to-govern-autonomous-agents-in-enterprise-ai-factories/) NVIDIA published a reference design for running autonomous agents in the enterprise that treats governance as a runtime control enforced before each action. The rules are concrete: ["Block all internet traffic by default"](https://developer.nvidia.com/blog/how-to-govern-autonomous-agents-in-enterprise-ai-factories/) with an explicit allowlist; a ["secure proxy that handles those keys behind the scenes so the agent never sees the raw secrets"](https://developer.nvidia.com/blog/how-to-govern-autonomous-agents-in-enterprise-ai-factories/); signed policy bundles plus a check that ["the security rules are active and working before every single action the agent performs"](https://developer.nvidia.com/blog/how-to-govern-autonomous-agents-in-enterprise-ai-factories/); and human sign-off on ["any agent action that changes a system (like merging code or updating tickets)"](https://developer.nvidia.com/blog/how-to-govern-autonomous-agents-in-enterprise-ai-factories/).

## Why it matters
Agents are being deployed faster than the controls around them. A Google technical lead writing in [California Management Review](https://cmr.berkeley.edu/2026/03/governing-the-agentic-enterprise-a-new-operating-model-for-autonomous-ai-at-scale/) calls it the "Orchestration Gap," where "decentralized software outpaces centralized human management." An audit log tells you what a rogue action did; only a check before the action can stop it.

## How it works
1. **Least privilege, per agent.** Give each agent its own identity and default-deny access — AWS makes the same point: ["Agents require their own identities, secure credential storage, and least-privilege authorization"](https://aws.amazon.com/blogs/security/four-security-principles-for-agentic-ai-systems/).
2. **Enforce in the execution path.** Microsoft's open-source Agent Governance Toolkit ["intercepts every agent's action before execution"](https://opensource.microsoft.com/blog/2026/04/02/introducing-the-agent-governance-toolkit-open-source-runtime-security-for-ai-agents/) — the check sits inline in the execution path, at sub-millisecond latency.
3. **Human-in-the-loop for mutations.** Route any state-changing action to a person; AWS reserves this for ["actions that could modify high-value production data, initiate financial transactions, or communicate sensitive information externally"](https://aws.amazon.com/blogs/security/four-security-principles-for-agentic-ai-systems/).

> Governance you can't enforce before the action is just a record of what went wrong.

## The catch
This is NVIDIA describing its own reference stack, and "AI factory" is its framing — read it as one vendor's product architecture rather than a neutral standard. But the load-bearing ideas — default-deny, credential proxy, signed policy, pre-action checks, human approval — show up independently at [AWS](https://aws.amazon.com/blogs/security/four-security-principles-for-agentic-ai-systems/) and [Microsoft](https://opensource.microsoft.com/blog/2026/04/02/introducing-the-agent-governance-toolkit-open-source-runtime-security-for-ai-agents/), which is what makes them worth copying no matter whose product you buy.

[Security](/guide/security/)
